tidyMacro 1.0.0

tidyMacro

High-Performance Vector Autoregression in R

Muhsin Ciftci

2026-03-25

Source: vignettes/index.qmd

tidyMacro is an R package for fast estimation of Structural Vector Autoregression (SVAR) models. The core is written in C++ via RcppArmadillo, exposing parallelized bootstraps through OpenMP for maximum performance.

devtools::install_github("muhsinciftci/tidyMacro")

1 Key Features

  • Zero extra dependencies — all econometrics implemented from scratch in C++
  • Publication-ready plots — every output is a ggplot2 object, fully customizable after the fact
  • Parallel bootstraps — OpenMP-backed; uses n−1n-1 cores by default (n_threads = 1 to disable)
  • Tidy-first workflow — import and clean data with tidyverse, pass to C++ via as.matrix()

2 Identification Methods

2.1 Recursive (Cholesky) Ordering

The most common identification approach. The lower-triangular Cholesky factor of the residual covariance matrix Σ\Sigma is used as the structural impact matrix:

B0−1=PwhereΣ=PP′B_0^{-1} = P \quad \text{where} \quad \Sigma = PP'

Variables ordered first are assumed to respond contemporaneously to those ordered later. Confidence bands are computed via residual or wild bootstraps, with an optional bias-corrected variant following Kilian (1998).

Relevant functions: fVAR(), fwoldIRF(), fcholeskyIRF(), fbootstrapChol(), fbootstrapCholCorrected(), fplotirf_chol(), fevd_chol(), fhistdec()

2.2 Long-Run Restrictions (Blanchard–Quah)

Identification is achieved by imposing that certain shocks have zero long-run effect on selected variables. The long-run impact matrix is

C(1)=(I−A1−⋯−Ap)−1B0−1C(1) = (I - A_1 - \cdots - A_p)^{-1} B_0^{-1}

and its lower-triangular Cholesky factorization pins down the structural matrix.

Relevant functions: fbqIRF(), fbootstrapBQ(), fplotirf_bq()

2.3 External Instruments (Proxy-SVAR)

An external instrument ztz_t — correlated with the target structural shock but orthogonal to all others — is used to construct the first-stage projection following Mertens & Ravn (2013) and Stock & Watson (2012):

ε̂1t=Πzt+residual\hat{\varepsilon}_{1t} = \Pi z_t + \text{residual}

The identified shock is then scaled to give unit impact. Confidence bands use moving-block bootstraps (MBB).

Relevant functions: fVAR(), fbqIRF(), fbootstrapBQ(), fplotirf_bq()

3 Replications

The package ships with four published datasets and step-by-step replications.

Study Identification Key Result
Bloom (2009) Recursive ordering Uncertainty shocks cause sharp, short-lived recessions
Galí (1999) Long-run restrictions Technology shocks reduce hours in the short run
Beaudry & Portier (2014) Long-run restrictions News shocks drive comovement in investment and consumption
Kaenzig (2021) External instrument OPEC announcements shift oil prices and propagate globally

4 Full Tutorial

The main vignette walks through every step: data import, VAR estimation, lag selection, impulse responses, variance decomposition, and historical decomposition.
